title: On the Distortion of Partitioning Performance by Random Quantum Circuits
abstract: Hypergraph partitioning is a central component of distributed quantum computing (DQC) compilers. However, due to the limited size of available quantum benchmark suites, many partitioning studies rely on random quantum circuits as evaluation workloads. In this work, we investigate whether such benchmarking practices provide a faithful assessment of partitioner performance.   We evaluate a diverse set of state-of-the-art hypergraph partitioning strategies across three circuit origins: real algorithmic circuits, structured generated circuits, and fully random circuits.   Our results show that random circuits significantly distort partitioning evaluation. They inflate cut costs, alter scaling trends across QPU counts and circuit sizes, and change the relative ranking of partitioning strategies. In contrast, structured generated circuits exhibit substantially lower distortion, more closely approximating real workload behaviour in cost, scaling, and strategy rankings. These findings demonstrate that benchmark selection directly influences methodological conclusions in DQC research and that random circuits may provide misleading guidance for compiler design.
summary: This paper demonstrates that using random quantum circuits for benchmarking DQC compilers leads to misleading conclusions about the effectiveness of partitioning algorithms. It shows that structured, generated circuits are a much more reliable proxy for real-world quantum workloads.
main_problem: Investigating whether using random quantum circuits as benchmarks provides a faithful assessment of hypergraph partitioning performance in Distributed Quantum Computing (DQC) compilers.
main_result: Random circuits significantly distort partitioning evaluations by inflating cut costs, altering scaling trends, and changing the relative ranking of partitioning strategies, whereas structured generated circuits (QGen) closely approximate real workloads.
method: The study evaluates various partitioning strategies (e.g., KaHyPar, Zoltan PHG, FM, StochG) across three circuit ensembles: Real, Random, and Generated (QGen), using statistical tools like Spearman rank correlation and Mann-Whitney U tests.
model_or_system: A Distributed Quantum Computing (DQC) model where a large workload is distributed across a network of k smaller, fully connected Quantum Processing Units (QPUs), represented as hypergraphs where qubits are vertices and multi-qubit gates are hyperedges.
key_observables: Cut cost (number of hyperedges spanning different partitions), scaling trends (relative to QPU count and circuit size), and strategy ranking (relative performance order of algorithms).
important_parameters: Number of partitions (k from 2 to 10), circuit size (n up to 134 qubits), number of multi-qubit gates (up to 20,000), and imbalance tolerance (5%).
main_assumptions: The inter-QPU network is assumed to be fully connected, and the study assumes that qubit interaction topology is the primary driver of partitioning performance.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows total cut costs across partitioners; Figure 2 shows performance variance heatmaps; Figure 3 shows normalized cut cost vs. number of QPUs; Figure 4 shows normalized cut cost; Figure 5 uses violin plots to illustrate the distribution of total cut costs.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the DQC partitioning problem, defines the three circuit ensembles (Real, Random, Generated), details the partitioning algorithms and evaluation metrics, presents comparative results regarding cost and ranking stability, and concludes with implications for compiler design and future work.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Total cut cost produced by each partitioning strategy across real, generated, and random circuit origins.
Figure 2: Heatmap of partitioning performance spread across circuit origins and strategies.

Figure 3: Normalised cut cost (relative to the random assignment baseline) as a function of the number of QPUs. tapers off beyond approximately 100 qubits across techniques. FM again yields the highest cost in this category. Generated circuits follow a broadly similar trend, but with significantly higher variance across sizes, particularly below 20 qubits. They do not display the same pronounced stabilisation as real circuits at larger scales. Random circuits show a steady increase in cost with circuit size, progressively approaching the random baseline. This behaviour differs from that observed in real circuits, where cost stabilises rather than grows. Notably, random circuits are the only...

Figure 5 shows the aggregated cut cost distributions across all network sizes, partitioners, and circuit sizes. Generated circuits closely track the distribution of real circuits, exhibit- ing substantial overlap in both spread and central tendency. Notably, unlike real circuits, random circuits display broader dispersion and weaker overlap with the other two origins.
Figure 4: Normalised cut cost (relative to the random assignment baseline) as a function of circuit size (number of qubits).
